content: Welcome to Wenjun Zhang's homepage! I am a graduate student in the Bernien Lab at the University of Chicago. My research interests include atomic, molecular and optical (AMO) physics and quantum simulation. With Rydberg atom array, I'm excited to investigate the physics of quantum many-body systems.
summary: Welcome to Wenjun Zhang's homepage! I am a graduate student in the Bernien Lab at the University of Chicago. My research interests include atomic, molecular and optical (AMO) physics and quantum simulation. With Rydberg atom array, I'm excited to investigate the physics of quantum many-body systems.
title: Wenjun Zhang

content:
I had never expected this pandemic spreading all over the globe and keeping all of us stuck in home. No one had. By the time I write down this post, it seems to have been 9 months since I worked in a lab last time. In the 2020 Spring semester, the very last semester of my undergraduate life, we have to prepare our bachelor thesis at home (with poor access to all the journals!), and change from an experimental subject to a theoretical one. This morning, when I routinely checked in my PKU portal to fill in the fighting-the-pandemic sheet, I couldn't find it anywhere. The portal has been modified from a student account to an alumni one. It is said that we don't have access to the PKU disk anymore, either. Luckily I'm not a frequent user. It was exactly this moment when I realized, I graduated. I believe this is gonna be the most special and unforgettable graduation. Thanks to all the doctors, nurses, and volunteers, and all those heros whose names I don't know, we're getting back to a more or less normal life, though we'd still better wear face masks. Thank you all to the ‘soldiers’ fighting in the frontline against the pandemic!
Actually, staying at home is not that bad. I have to say that I indeed acquire something thanks to this staying-at-home thing. Earlier this year when I worked as an undergraduate research assistant at MIT, two of my friends who are Ph.D. students at the Research Laboratory of Electronics, shared with me that every physics student should know something about the general relativity and the quantum field theory. I didn't have many ideas about this at that time. In this half a year, I followed a course in GR at PKU. I really like the idea of describing the whole world from a geometrical point of view. Both the interesting physical intuition and the beautiful mathematical structure impressed me. I also learned something about the QFT by myself. Though there are many techniques introduced in the texts, I don't feel like getting too much insights. I even thought the QFT was nothing but a bunch of dirty tools, which is certainly not true. Recently I am doing a survey on the quantum spin liquids. I instantaneously find myself quite interested in the topological order or the more general concept quantum order. I read some papers by Prof. Xiao-Gang Wen at MIT and really liked them. This leads me to reading his texts, Quantum Field Theory of Many Body Systems From the Origin of Sound to an Origin of Light and Electrons. I am really fascinated by his explanation and his emphasis the origin of our fundamental particles. They could be just low-energy excitations of some unknown field theory. And our universe could be nothing but a vacuum state that we're familiar with in the condensed matter context. These two things made me again curious about the origin of our universe.
Okay. That's all for my first post. It is more like a diary. But whatever, I'll put up some of my notes on the quantum spin liquids, which is more research oriented.
summary: General relativity and QFT get me excited again!
title: Graduation

summary: As in between liquid and crystal phases lies a nematic liquid crystal, which breaks rotation with preservation of translation symmetry, there is a nematic superfluid phase bridging a superfluid and a supersolid. The nematic order also emerges in interacting electrons and has been found to largely intertwine with multiorbital correlation in high-temperature superconductivity, where Ising nematicity arises from a four-fold rotation symmetry $C_4$ broken down to $C_2$. Here, we report an observation of a three-state ($Z_3$) quantum nematic order, dubbed "Potts-nematicity", in a system of cold atoms loaded in an excited band of a hexagonal optical lattice described by an $sp^2$-orbital hybridized model. This Potts-nematic quantum state spontaneously breaks a three-fold rotation symmetry of the lattice, qualitatively distinct from the Ising nematicity. Our field theory analysis shows that the Potts-nematic order is stabilized by intricate renormalization effects enabled by strong interorbital mixing present in the hexagonal lattice. This discovery paves a way to investigate quantum vestigial orders in multiorbital atomic superfluids.
title: Evidence of Potts-Nematic Superfluidity in a Hexagonal $sp^2$ Optical Lattice

summary: We realize a double-path multi-mode matter wave interferometer with spinor Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) and observe clear spatial interference fringes as well as a periodic change of the visibility in the time domain, which we refer to as the time domain interference and is different from the traditional double-path interferometer. By changing the relative phase of the two paths, we find that the spatial fringes first lose coherence and recover. As the number of modes increases, the time domain interference signal with the narrower peaks is observed, which is beneficial to the improvement of the resolution of the phase measurement. We also investigated the influence of initial phase configuration and phase evolution rate between different modes in the two paths. With enhanced resolution, the sensitivity of interferometric measurements of physical observables can also be improved by properly assigning measurable quantities to the relative phase between two paths.
title: Implementation of a Double-Path Multi-Mode Interferometer Using a Spinor Bose-Einstein Condensate

summary: Ultracold atoms in periodical-driven optical lattices enable us to investigate novel band structures and explore the topology of the bands. In this work, we investigate the impact of the ramping process of the driving signal and propose a simple but effective method to realize desired asymmetric population in momentum distribution by controlling the initial phase of the driving signal. A quasi-momentum oscillation along the shaking direction in the frame of reference co-moving with the lattice is formed, causing the formation of the mix of ground energy band and first excited band in laboratory frame, within the regime that the driving frequency is far less than the coupling frequency between ground band and higher energy bands. This method avoids the construction of intricate lattices or complex control sequence. With a triangular lattice, we experimentally investigate the influence of the initial phase, frequency, amplitude of the driving signal on the population difference and observe good agreement with our theoretical model. This provides guidance on how to load a driving signal in driven optical lattice experiment and also potentially supplies a useful tool to form a qubit that can be used in quantum computation.
title: Asymmetric Population of Momentum Distribution by Quasi-Periodically Driving a Triangular Optical Lattice
